title: Tahdig

overview.description: Persian steamed rice with a golden, crisp layer of rice formed at the bottom of the pot.

ingredients: 2 tbsp salt, for the cooking water
method: Rinse the rice repeatedly in cold water until the water runs mostly clear.
Place the rice in a large bowl and cover with cold water.
Add 1 tbsp salt and soak for at least 1 hour.
Bring a large pot of water to a vigorous boil.
Add the remaining salt.
Drain the soaked rice and add it to the boiling water.
Boil for approximately 5–7 minutes until the outside of the grains is soft but the centre remains firm.
Drain the rice thoroughly.
Rinse briefly with warm water to remove excess starch.
Combine the oil, butter or ghee and water in a heavy-based non-stick saucepan.
Heat over medium heat.
Add approximately one-quarter of the rice to the bottom of the pan.
Gently spread it into an even layer.
Add the remaining rice, piling it loosely into a mound.
Use the handle of a wooden spoon to make several holes through the rice to allow steam to escape.
Wrap the lid in a clean tea towel and place it firmly on the pan.
Cook over medium heat for approximately 8–10 minutes until steam begins to build.
Reduce the heat to very low.
Cook for a further 35–45 minutes.
Remove from the heat and allow the pan to stand for 5 minutes.
Carefully loosen the rice around the edges.
Invert the pan onto a large serving platter so the crisp tahdig is revealed.
Alternatively, spoon the rice onto a platter and serve the tahdig separately in pieces.

learning.did-you-know: Tahdig means 'bottom of the pot' in Persian. The crisp layer is often considered the best part of the meal and is commonly shared among the diners.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How parboiling and steaming produce fluffy rice while controlled heat creates a crisp, golden layer at the bottom of the pot.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A fascinating rice technique that transforms a familiar pantry staple into something completely different, while introducing one of the defining pleasures of Persian cuisine.
chapman-tips: A good non-stick saucepan makes the first attempt much easier.
The rice should be undercooked in the centre when it is drained after boiling.
Don't pack the rice tightly into the pan; steam needs to circulate.
The tea towel absorbs condensation and helps prevent soggy rice.
Low heat is essential once the rice begins steaming.
Don't keep lifting the lid to check the tahdig.
If the tahdig is sticking, place the bottom of the pan briefly in cold water before attempting to invert it.
The first attempt may be lighter or darker than expected; heat control is the main variable to learn.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The crisp texture is lost during refrigeration and reheating.
notes.leftovers: The fluffy rice reheats well, but the tahdig is best eaten immediately.
